nativeRange.habitat: The Tube Coral is found on shaded reef walls, steep slopes, caves, crevices, and overhangs across the Indo-Pacific, and has also been documented in mesophotic and deep-reef habitats where light is minimal. It is adapted to environments with strong currents and has no dependence on photosynthesis for energy.

decorPreferences: stable rockwork base away from shifting sand

diet.notes: The Tube Coral is entirely heterotrophic, meaning it contains no zooxanthellae (the symbiotic algae that allow most reef corals to feed via photosynthesis) and derives no energy from light whatsoever. It must be fed directly. Daily target-feeding of meaty zooplankton to each individual polyp is the single most critical aspect of its care: frozen mysis shrimp, vitamin-enriched brine shrimp, copepods, rotifers, and finely chopped clam or scallop are generally accepted. A turkey baster, pipette, or dosing syringe is strongly recommended to direct food to each polyp opening individually. Live copepods (e.g., Tigriopus californicus) have been reported to be particularly effective. Amino acid supplementation can assist overall nutrition but does not substitute for regular particulate target-feeding.

goodWith: other non-photosynthetic corals (Tubastraea, Dendrophyllia, Chili Coral, Sea Fans)
avoidWith: aggressive corals with long sweeper tentacles (Galaxea, Hammer, Torch)
fast-growing photosynthetic corals that may overgrow
algae-prone areas where overgrowth may smother the base
food-stealing fish (wrasses, triggers, large angelfish) near feeding zone

imageCandidates.notes: Photographer Prilfish; 3000x4000. In-situ colony showing both retracted red-orange tube skeletons and vivid extended golden-orange feeding polyps with tentacles — best single image of the diagnostic tube-like skeleton plus vibrant NPS coloration. Filed under basionym Dendrophyllia gracilis (= Cladopsammia gracilis).

Photographer Prilfish; 4000x3000. Tack-sharp close-up of fully extended, translucent golden-orange polyps — striking full-colour NPS look. Tight cluster crop, kept as strong non-recommended extra. Basionym Dendrophyllia gracilis.
Photographer Diego Delso; 4229x4229. Sharp in-situ shot of yellow-tentacled polyps with orange oral discs on tube skeletons — shows a yellow colour form of the species.
Photographer Diego Delso; 5205x3894. Yellow morph, several polyps with tube skeleton bases clearly visible; crisp reef-wall in-situ image.

Photographer Diego Delso; 7598x5065. Vivid pink-and-orange fluorescent tube skeletons under strobe/reef lighting — best display of the branching tube-like calcium carbonate form; polyps mostly retracted.
Photographer Diego Delso; 5920x3947. Pink colour-form colony with orange-centred polyps semi-open on tube skeletons; sharp in-situ.

Photographer Silke Baron; 1943x1450. Orange tube-coral colony with polyps retracted, showing skeleton form plus associated Epidendrium snail egg mass; lower-ranked extra. Basionym Dendrophyllia gracilis.

summary: The Tube Coral is a small non-photosynthetic stony coral (Cladopsammia sp.) with vivid orange polyps and narrow, tube-like calcium carbonate skeletons. It derives no energy from light, making daily target-feeding to each individual polyp the single most critical care requirement. Advanced care.
careNotes:
Tube Corals belong to the family Dendrophylliidae — the same family as the well-known Tubastraea sun corals — and are classified as non-photosynthetic large polyp stony (LPS) corals. They contain no zooxanthellae (the symbiotic algae that power most reef-building corals through photosynthesis) and cannot derive any energy from light. The most commonly traded aquarium species is believed to be Cladopsammia gracilis, a small colonial coral with characteristic narrow, tube-like calcium carbonate skeletons and bright orange polyps.

FEEDING — THE CRITICAL REQUIREMENT: Daily target-feeding to each individual polyp is the single most important husbandry factor. Most aquarium losses of Dendrophylliidae non-photosynthetic corals are attributed to underfeeding or inconsistent feeding schedules. Polyps tend to extend naturally in the evening or when they sense food in the water column, but can be conditioned to open during daytime with consistent feeding routines. Use a turkey baster, pipette, or dosing syringe to direct frozen mysis, brine shrimp, copepods, or finely chopped marine seafood directly at each polyp opening. Never allow feeding gaps longer than 1–2 days.

LIGHTING: Requires no light for energy — placement in the darkest zones of the aquarium is acceptable and often preferred. PAR (a measure of light intensity) levels under 75 are generally advised; high-light zones are best avoided to prevent tissue stress.

FLOW: Moderate to high flow delivers planktonic food and carries away waste from the colony's feeding activity. Briefly reducing flow in the immediate vicinity during feeding sessions can improve capture rates.

PLACEMENT: Can be placed anywhere in the tank including inverted under overhangs or on cave ceilings — a placement frequently used in expert non-photosynthetic coral tanks. Avoid positioning on or near sandy substrates where sediment movement can abrade and damage tissue. Elevate on stable, secure rockwork.

NUTRIENT LOAD: Regular heavy feeding introduces substantial organic waste. An oversized protein skimmer and consistent water changes are generally advised to manage nitrate and phosphate accumulation.

SAME-FAMILY REFERENCE: Husbandry is closely analogous to Tubastraea sun corals [tubastraea-sun-coral] and Dendrophyllia. The daily target-feeding requirement and placement preferences are shared across the Dendrophylliidae family in captivity.

FRAGGING: Colonies can be fragmented with bone cutters; individual polyps with attached skeleton will typically regrow given adequate feeding.
breedingNotes: Cladopsammia reproduce via broadcast spawning in the wild. In captivity, fragmentation with bone cutters is the most practical propagation method; polyp clusters with attached skeleton will encrust and grow provided feeding is maintained. Sexual reproduction in captivity has not been reliably documented for this genus.

primary.name: LiveAquaria — Tube Coral (Orange)
primary.notes: Primary source for care parameters: temperature 72–78°F (22.2–25.6°C), salinity 1.023–1.025, pH 8.1–8.4, dKH 8–12, flow medium to strong, placement bottom, care level moderate. Note: product listed as Tubastraea aurea (Orange Tube Coral); Tubastraea and Cladopsammia are both Dendrophylliidae NPS corals with near-identical aquarium husbandry. Used as primary care reference in absence of dedicated Cladopsammia retail pages. Care level elevated to 'advanced' per controller spec and feeding-burden analogy with sibling entry tubastraea-sun-coral (sw-coral-091).

additional.name: WoRMS — Cladopsammia gracilis
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (Family Dendrophylliidae, Order Scleractinia), native range (Palau, Singapore, Australia, Marshall Islands, Ecuador EEZs), depth range 0–95 m, and confirmed azooxanthellate status. Original description: Dendrophyllia gracilis Milne Edwards & Haime, 1848.

additional.name: Wikipedia — Cladopsammia
additional.notes: Confirmed taxonomy (Family Dendrophylliidae, Order Scleractinia), azooxanthellate status, and deep-reef habitat adaptation. Lists 6+ recognized species including C. gracilis. Used for taxonomy and species identification cross-check only.

additional.name: AlgaeBarn — Keeping and Feeding the Sun Corals
additional.notes: Cross-reference for Dendrophylliidae NPS coral care (primarily Tubastraea). Confirmed: no lighting required, strong flow for food delivery, daily live copepod (Tigriopus californicus) feeding highly effective, cave/overhang placement recommended. Used to support feeding and placement guidance applicable to all Dendrophylliidae NPS corals.

additional.name: AquaMate sibling entry — Tubastraea Sun Coral (sw-coral-091)
additional.notes: Cross-reference sibling: same family Dendrophylliidae, same NPS azooxanthellate classification, same daily target-feeding pattern. Water parameters (temp 24–26°C, SG 1.023–1.025, pH 8.1–8.4, kH 8–12, Ca 400–450 ppm, Mg 1250–1400 ppm), PAR 0–75, flow high, placement anywhere, careLevel advanced adopted consistently. Colony size used as upper bound reference (15 cm); Cladopsammia is notably smaller than Tubastraea, adult max set at 13 cm.
